title: Solving models with generalized free fermions II: Path-product expansion and conserved charges
authors: Kohei Fukai, Balázs Pozsgay, István Vona
abstract: Free-fermion solvability in quantum spin systems is increasingly understood to be governed by a graph Clifford algebra defined from the frustration graph of the Hamiltonian. When the frustration graph belongs to certain classes, such as the even-hole-free and claw-free (ECF) class, the Hamiltonian is solvable by hidden free fermions: it admits a free-fermion solution although it does not reduce to a Majorana bilinear under the Jordan-Wigner transformation. However, unlike in the Jordan-Wigner case, where each mode is a linear combination of single Majorana fermions, the explicit operator structure of the hidden free-fermion modes -- and that of the local conserved charges -- has remained obscure. In this work, we derive a path-product expansion that expresses each free-fermion mode as a linear combination of path products along induced paths in the extended frustration graph. The expansion is obtained from the generating function of the Krylov basis and yields the modes directly, without using the transfer matrix or the nonlocal conserved charges as input. As an application, the mode decomposition computes infinite-temperature dynamical correlation functions for arbitrary ECF frustration graphs. We further obtain explicit expressions for local conserved charges as linear combinations of path products along induced paths; these charges apply beyond the free-fermion (ECF) class to more general claw-free frustration graphs. We also identify a unified family of generalized conserved charges that contains both the previously known nonlocal conserved charges and these local conserved charges as special cases. For Fendley's original FFD chain with homogeneous couplings and periodic boundary conditions, in a suitable basis, the structure of these local conserved charges exhibits the same Catalan-tree pattern as in the spin-$1/2$ XXX chain.
summary: This work develops a path-product expansion technique to explicitly determine the hidden free-fermion modes and local conserved charges in quantum spin systems defined on frustration graphs. By utilizing the Krylov basis generating function, the authors establish a direct link between graph structure and integrability, providing a unified framework for understanding conserved quantities in these models.
main_problem: To derive an explicit, graph-theoretic structure for the hidden free-fermion modes and local conserved charges in quantum spin systems solvable by free fermions.
main_result: The free-fermion modes are shown to be expressible as path-product expansions along induced paths, and local conserved charges are determined by path products along induced paths with an odd number of vertices.
method: The authors employ a path-product expansion derived from the generating function of the Krylov basis, bypassing the need for the transfer matrix or nonlocal charges.
model_or_system: The study focuses on quantum spin systems defined on a frustration graph, particularly those belonging to the Even-Hole-Free and Claw-Free (ECF) classes. The Hamiltonian is governed by a graph Clifford algebra.
key_observables: Free-fermion modes, local conserved charges, and infinite-temperature dynamical correlation functions.
important_parameters: The structure of the frustration graph (ECF or claw-free); the path length $|L|$ of induced paths.
main_assumptions: The graph must be ECF for the primary mode derivation, but the charge construction extends to general claw-free graphs.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate claw obstructions and the structure of the extended frustration graph ($G_\chi$) used in the path-product expansion.
paper_structure: The paper develops the framework by first introducing the graph-Clifford algebra, then deriving the path-product expansion for the free-fermion modes using the Krylov basis generating function, and finally applying this to derive explicit local and generalized conserved charges.
why_interesting: The explicit, graph-theoretic construction of conserved charges provides a powerful, general tool for analyzing integrability in complex quantum spin models, applicable beyond standard lattice symmetries.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 The claw graph K1,3, consisting of a center vertex connected to three mutually non-adjacent leaves.

Fig. 2 The two claw obstructions used in Lemma 3.3. In (a), an interior path vertex ℓi touched by j but not accompanied by either neighboring touched vertex would have three pairwise non-adjacent neighbors ℓi−1, ℓi+1, j. In (b), three touched path vertices no two of which are consecutive along the induced path are pairwise non-adjacent, so they form a claw with center j. The double-circled vertex marks the center of the forbidden claw in each panel. Black dotted path segments indicate omitted continuations of the induced path.

Fig. 5 The (2, 1) mixed-touch cancellation. The filled vertex is the Hamiltonian vertex of the commutator term: j in panel (a) and j′ in panel (b). Black dotted segments indicate omitted continuations of induced paths. Gray dashed edges indicate touching edges from the Hamiltonian vertex to the packed components. Yellow and blue halos mark the doubly touched side and the singly touched side, respectively. The braces label the path pieces LO, LE, L′, and L′ E used in the decompositions before and after the move. In the left panel the doubly touched component is denoted by L = LE-LO, where LE = L′ E-j′, while the singly touched component is denoted by L′. In the right panel the doubly touched...

Fig. 6 Schematic one-component touch with spectators for c = 4. Only packing vertices and the Hamiltonian vertex are drawn; unrelated vertices and edges of the ambient graph are suppressed. The gray and red halos schematically indicate the closed neighborhoods of the spectator components and of the active component, respectively. Distinct halos may themselves overlap, but the packing condition Γ[La] ∩Lb = ∅for a ̸= b requires that no vertex of one packed component lie in the halo of another. Fig. 7 Extended frustration graph Gχ for the Ising algebra, shown for the case M = 7. In general, the frustration graph G = PM is the path graph with M = 2L −1 vertices (white), where adjacent vertices correspond to anticommuting generators. The edge vertex χ (black) is connected to the simplicial clique Ks = {1}.
